source_book: NCERT Class 11 Physics Part I Ch.8 + HC Verma Ch.5

real_world_anchor.primary: A mango dangles from a thread tied to the ceiling. The thread pulls upward on the mango. Cut the thread and it falls — the upward pull is gone.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A bucket of water rises from a well. The rope pulls the bucket up; by Newton's third law, the bucket pulls the rope down. That mutual pull is the tension.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: An Atwood machine has unequal masses on two ends of a single rope. The system accelerates, but the tension has the same value on both sides — why?

formulas.single_hanging_mass_acceleration: T = m*(g - a) for upward acceleration

constraints: A string can only PULL — it cannot push.
String tension acts AWAY from the body, along the direction of the string.
If the string breaks or goes slack, T = 0 instantly.
A rod can both push and pull; a string can only pull.
Inextensible string -> same acceleration magnitude for all connected masses.
Massless string -> tension is uniform along its length.
Massless frictionless pulley -> tension is the same on both sides.

string_rules.name: Inextensible string
string_rules.statement: If the string is inextensible, the acceleration magnitude is the same for all masses connected by it.

string_rules.name: Massless string
string_rules.statement: If the string is massless, the tension is the same at every point along it.

string_rules.name: Massless, frictionless pulley
string_rules.statement: If the pulley is massless and frictionless, the tension is the same on both sides of the pulley.

STATE_1.title: Mango Hanging from a Thread — What Is Holding It?

scene_composition.text:
Something must be pulling it up —
otherwise it would fall.
Where does that pull come from?

tts_sentences.text_en: A mango hangs from a thread attached to the ceiling. Gravity pulls it downward with a force of nine point eight newtons.
Yet it does not fall. Something upward must be balancing gravity.
That upward force comes from the thread. But how exactly does a thread apply a force?
We call that force tension. Today we will see what tension really is and where it comes from.

STATE_2.title: Cut the String — The Force Vanishes. Now We Know.

right_scene.text: T = 0. No string means no tension.

scene_composition.text: Definition: the pulling force along a taut string.
Cutting the string removed exactly one thing — the upward force T. That upward force is the tension.

tts_sentences.text_en: Imagine cutting the string.
The mango immediately starts to fall because the upward force has vanished.
That missing upward force was the tension. It lived inside the string.
At equilibrium, tension equals weight. T equals mg equals nine point eight newtons, pointing straight up along the string.

STATE_3.title: Rule 1: Inextensible String — Same Acceleration Everywhere

scene_composition.text:
Inextensible means length cannot change.
Both ends move at the same speed.
So both blocks share the same acceleration a.

tts_sentences.text_en: Rule one. The string is inextensible, meaning its length cannot stretch.
If the string cannot stretch, both of its ends must always move at the same speed.
Equal speed means equal acceleration. So M and m accelerate together with the same value of a.
This is how we build the constraint equation that links two otherwise separate blocks.

STATE_4.title: Rules 2 and 3: Massless String, Massless Pulley — Tension Is Uniform

left_scene.text:
No mass inside the string ->
no extra force is needed
to accelerate string itself.

right_scene.text:
Upper part also has to pull
the lower string's own mass.
Tension is larger at the top.

scene_composition.text: Rule 3: Pulley massless and frictionless -> T is the same on both sides.

tts_sentences.text_en: Rule two. In a massless string, the tension is the same at every point along the string.
The reason is simple. A massless string needs no extra force to accelerate itself.
Rule three. Across a massless frictionless pulley, the tension is the same on both sides.
These three rules together let you treat the tension as a single unknown that lives throughout the string. Tap continue.
The Atwood machine has two masses hanging from one string over a massless pulley.
The heavier mass m one falls while the lighter m two rises. Both share the same tension T and the same acceleration a.
Apply Newton's second law to each block. Eliminate T between the two equations.
The result is a beautiful formula: T equals two m one m two g divided by m one plus m two — the harmonic mean of the two weights.

STATE_6.title: You Control It — Change Masses, See T and a Live

tts_sentences.text_en: Drag both mass sliders and watch the system respond.
Set m one equal to m two. The acceleration goes to zero, and T equals mg — the system is in equilibrium.
Increase m one. The acceleration grows, and T sits somewhere below m one g — never above it.
The tension is always between m one g and m two g. It can never exceed the heavier weight.

panel_b_config.title: Tension vs Mass Ratio
panel_b_config.subtitle: T = 2*m1*m2*g / (m1+m2) — harmonic mean of m1*g and m2*g

annotations.text: T always lies between m1*g and m2*g.

regeneration_variants.label: Pulling a Bucket from a Well
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A rope pulls a bucket of water up from a well. If accelerating upward, T = m*(g + a). If decelerating, T = m*(g - a).
regeneration_variants.same_physics: A string can only pull. Tension depends on the system's acceleration.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Well opening, bucket hanging on rope, person pulling rope. If a is upward, T is greater than mg.

regeneration_variants.label: Three Blocks on a Smooth Surface
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Three blocks M, m1, m2 on a smooth horizontal surface connected by strings. A force F pulls the end block. Find the tension in the middle string.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Inextensible string means the same a for everything. Massless string means the same T throughout each segment.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Three blocks in a row, connected by two strings, external force on the rightmost. Solve for tension in the middle string.

regeneration_variants.label: Conical Pendulum
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A ball tied to a string swings in a horizontal circle. The string traces a cone. Tension has vertical component balancing gravity and horizontal component providing centripetal force.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: A string can only pull along its length. Resolve T into components to handle 2D motion.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: String attached to a point above, ball moving in a horizontal circle. Show T along the string, mg down. T*cos(theta) = mg, T*sin(theta) = m*v^2/r.

epic_c_branches.misconception: A string can also push — it can exert a compression force.

left_scene.text:
Taut string: T > 0 (pull only).
Slack string: T = 0.
Compressing a string is impossible — it just bends.

right_scene.text:
Rod pushed: compression works.
Rod pulled: tension works.
A rigid rod supports both directions.

scene_composition.text: A string buckles under compression — a rod does not.

Exam tip: whenever you see 'string', tension is >= 0.
A rigid 'rod' or 'rigid link' can have negative internal force (compression).

tts_sentences.text_en: This is the core difference between a string and a rigid rod.
A string can only pull. Try to push it and it simply buckles — it cannot carry a compression.
A rod is rigid. It can push and pull along its length.
In exam problems, whenever you see the word string, tension can only be zero or positive — never negative.

EPIC_C_2.title: When a String Goes Slack — T = 0, Not Negative

scene_composition.text:
Taut: T > 0.
The string pulls both ends toward each other.

Slack: T = 0.
No force is transmitted.
Tension cannot be negative. A negative T would mean pushing the ends apart — that is a rigid rod, not a string.

tts_sentences.text_en: The tension in a string is always non-negative. Either T is positive or T equals zero.
A zero tension simply means the string has gone slack. No force is transmitted.
If your calculation ever produces a negative T, your assumption about the direction of motion or about the string segment is wrong.
Revisit the diagram. Flip the assumed direction of acceleration or of the string segment, and redo the algebra.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The tension is different at different points along the string.
trigger_phrases: T1 at top is different from T2 at bottom

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: Tension Varies Along the String

scene_composition.text:
For a massless string: T1 = T2 = T3 = T (same everywhere).
For a massive, accelerating string: T1 > T2 > T3.

tts_sentences.text_en: This is a common confusion. Does the tension vary along the string, or not?
Answer: in a massless string, T is exactly the same at every point.
A massless string has no internal mass to accelerate, so no extra pulling is needed to carry any segment along.
If the string had real mass and the system were accelerating, tension would be larger at the top and smaller at the bottom.

EPIC_C_2.title: Proof: Massless String Element Has Zero Net Force

scene_composition.equation: F_net = T2 - T1 = m_element * a = 0 * a = 0  =>  T1 = T2

tts_sentences.text_en: Take a small element of the string. Its mass is zero.
Apply Newton's second law. Net force equals mass times acceleration. Mass is zero, so net force must be zero.
That forces T one and T two to be equal — the tensions on either side of the element must match.
This proof works everywhere inside the string, whether the string is straight or bent around a pulley.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Tension in a hanging string always equals mg, even when the system is accelerating.

right_scene.text:
Accelerating up -> T must exceed mg.
The extra pull produces the acceleration.

scene_composition.text: Myth: T = mg no matter what the lift is doing.

Newton's 2nd law says T - mg = m*a.
Only when a = 0 does T equal mg.
Otherwise T = m*(g + a) upward or m*(g - a) downward.

tts_sentences.text_en: Students often memorise T equals mg and then apply it blindly, even when the system is accelerating.
That formula only holds in equilibrium — when the acceleration is zero.
If a lift accelerates upward, the string must pull harder to produce that acceleration. T equals m times g plus a.
If the lift accelerates downward, the string can pull less. T equals m times g minus a, and if a equals g the string goes slack and T equals zero.

EPIC_C_2.title: Derivation: T Follows Newton's Second Law

scene_composition.equation: sum(Fy) = m*a  =>  T - mg = m*a  =>  T = m*(g + a)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the clean derivation. Isolate the mass and draw the free body diagram.
Two forces: T upward and mg downward. Apply Newton's second law in the y direction.
T minus mg equals m a. Rearranging, T equals m times g plus a.
For an upward acceleration, a is positive and T exceeds mg. For a downward acceleration, a is negative and T is less than mg.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The tension is different on the two sides of a pulley because the masses differ.
trigger_phrases: T1 on left and T2 on right

EPIC_C_1.title: Ideal Pulley: T Is the Same on Both Sides

left_scene.text: Feels intuitive — but violates pulley physics.

right_scene.text: I = 0 and alpha finite -> T_left = T_right.

scene_composition.text:
If the pulley has mass (I > 0) or friction, then T_left != T_right.
Board-exam problems usually say 'massless, frictionless pulley' so you can treat T as uniform.

tts_sentences.text_en: It feels natural that the heavier side should 'carry' more tension. But the physics says otherwise for an ideal pulley.
Apply Newton's rotational law to the pulley. Net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration.
For a massless pulley, moment of inertia is zero. So the net torque must be zero, which forces the two tensions to be equal.
When the problem gives a pulley with mass, this no longer holds and you must write separate tensions on the two sides.

STATE_1.description: Identify the hanging mass, draw FBD with mg downward and T upward, state T as the pulling force along the string.

STATE_2.description: Apply equilibrium: sum(Fy) = 0 -> T = mg. State that cutting the string removes T and the mass falls under gravity alone.

STATE_3.description: State Rule 1: inextensible string -> both ends share the same acceleration magnitude. Write constraint |a1| = |a2| = a.

STATE_4.description: State Rules 2 and 3: massless string -> uniform T; massless frictionless pulley -> T equal on both sides. Justify using net-force-on-element argument.

STATE_5.description: Atwood machine: write Newton's law for each mass, eliminate T to get a = (m1-m2)*g/(m1+m2), then T = 2*m1*m2*g/(m1+m2).

primitives.text: Step 1: Hanging mass m on a taut thread; gravity acts downward.
Step 2: FBD: mg down, T up along the string.
Step 3: Tension acts AWAY from the body along the string.
+1 for FBD + T direction
Step 4: Equilibrium condition: sum(Fy) = 0.
Step 5: T - mg = 0  =>  T = mg.
Step 6: Cut string -> T = 0 -> mass falls freely (a = g).
+1 for T = mg derivation
Step 7: Rule 1 — inextensible string keeps its length constant.
Step 8: Therefore both ends have the same speed at all times.
Step 9: Constraint: |a1| = |a2| = a (common acceleration).
+1 for constraint equation
Step 10: Rule 2 — massless string element: F_net = 0 -> T uniform.
Step 11: Rule 3 — massless frictionless pulley: I = 0 -> T_left = T_right.
Step 12: Treat T as a single unknown for the whole system.
+1 for uniform-T justification
Step 13: m1*g - T = m1*a   (heavier mass falls)
Step 14: T - m2*g = m2*a   (lighter mass rises)
Step 15: Add -> a = (m1-m2)*g/(m1+m2); sub back -> T = 2*m1*m2*g/(m1+m2).
+1 for Atwood result

shortcuts.use_when: Body hangs at rest or moves at constant velocity (a = 0).

shortcuts.formula: T = m*(g + a)  (up)   or   m*(g - a)  (down)
shortcuts.use_when: Lift accelerates or decelerates; sign of a matches direction of the lift's acceleration.
Two masses over a single massless, frictionless pulley with an inextensible, massless string.
Same Atwood setup — sign tells which mass falls.
Quickly verifying an Atwood answer — T is always between the two weights (harmonic mean).
A mass swings in a horizontal circle; theta is the string's angle from vertical.

edge_cases.title: Pulley with non-zero moment of inertia
edge_cases.trap: Students keep T_left = T_right even though the pulley has mass.
edge_cases.resolution: Treat the pulley as a rotating body. (T_left - T_right)*R = I*alpha. Combined with a = R*alpha, the tensions become unequal.

edge_cases.title: Rope over a rough edge (capstan/belt friction)
edge_cases.trap: Students assume uniform tension despite the rough contact.
edge_cases.resolution: Tension changes exponentially: T2 / T1 = exp(mu * theta). Use the capstan equation when sliding is impending or occurring.

edge_cases.title: Finding the string angle from required speed
edge_cases.trap: Students treat theta as given when only v and length are known.
edge_cases.resolution: Use tan(theta) = v^2 / (r*g) with r = L*sin(theta); solve for theta numerically or by substitution.

edge_cases.title: System in free fall
edge_cases.trap: Students keep T = mg when the whole apparatus is falling.
edge_cases.resolution: If the entire system is in free fall (a = g downward), then in the co-falling frame, gravity is effectively zero. All strings go slack and T = 0 throughout.

edge_cases.title: Ball on a string at the top of a vertical circle
edge_cases.trap: Students forget that the minimum speed is set by T = 0, not by energy alone.
edge_cases.resolution: At the top, T + mg = m*v^2/r. Minimum v is when T = 0 -> v_min = sqrt(g*r). Below this speed, the string would need to push — impossible.
